title: Global resetting and emergent correlations: exit statistics in an interval
abstract: There is considerable current interest in the emergence of statistical correlations within a population of otherwise non-interacting Brownian particles subject to a common fluctuating environment or drive. Examples include global stochastic resetting, switching confining potentials, fluctuating diffusivities, and stochastically gated boundaries. Most studies have focused on the analytical structure of the stationary joint probability density (assuming it exists). In this paper, we extend previous work on the exit statistics of multiple particles in stochastically gated domains to the case of global resetting in an interval with absorbing boundaries at both ends. First, we use a generalised Itô's lemma to derive a hierarchy of boundary value problems (BVPs) for the joint splitting probability that all particles exit from the same end of the interval. The BVPs form a nested sequence with respect to the initial number of particles $M$. We explicitly solve the BVP for a pair of particles ($M=2$) and use this to illustrate the emergence of pairwise correlations. Second, we show how the BVP for the splitting probability of $M$ Brownian particles can be mapped onto the $M$th order moment equation of a stochastic diffusion equation with resetting. We thus establish a general mathematical framework to study exit problems for globally-driven particle systems.
summary: This paper explores how a shared stochastic resetting protocol induces correlations between independent Brownian particles. It develops a mathematical framework using nested boundary value problems to show that these particles no longer behave independently. The study establishes a direct link between multi-particle exit statistics and the moments of a stochastic diffusion equation.
main_problem: Investigating how global stochastic resetting induces statistical correlations between non-interacting Brownian particles and determining the resulting joint exit (splitting) probabilities.
main_result: The paper proves that global resetting creates emergent correlations such that the joint splitting probability cannot be decomposed into independent single-particle probabilities, and establishes a mapping between these probabilities and the moments of a stochastic diffusion equation.
method: The author uses generalized Ito's lemma, backward Kolmogorov equations, and Green's function methods to derive a hierarchy of nested boundary value problems (BVPs).
model_or_system: A population of M non-interacting Brownian particles in a 1D interval [0, L] with absorbing boundaries, subject to a global Poissonian resetting process to a common position.
key_observables: Splitting probability (probability of exiting through a specific boundary), joint splitting probability, and survival probability.
important_parameters: Resetting rate (r), diffusion coefficient (D), interval length (L), and the characteristic scale xi = sqrt(r/D).
main_assumptions: Particles are non-interacting (no physical collisions); resetting is global (all particles reset simultaneously); boundaries are strictly absorbing.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the global resetting mechanism; Figure 2 plots joint splitting probability vs. reset rate; Figure 4 compares global vs. local gating; Figure 5 compares joint splitting probabilities for global and local gating scenarios.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of emergent correlations, derives the BVP hierarchy using Ito's lemma, provides an analytical solution for the two-particle case, establishes a mapping to SPDE moment equations, and concludes with a comparison between global and local gating dynamics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to open quantum systems and many-body dynamics as it provides a mathematical framework for understanding how shared environmental noise (common-mode noise) generates entanglement-like statistical correlations in otherwise independent constituents.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Two examples of globally driven Brownian motion. (a) Pair of Brownian particles diﬀusing in R and subject to global resetting. At a random sequence of times Tn the positions of both particles are reset to the origin. The inter-event times τn = Tn+1 −Tn are exponentially distributed. (b) Pair of Brownian particles in the interval [0, L] with an open boundary at x = 0 and a stochastically gated boundary at x = L. The latter switches between an open state (blue) and a closed state (red) according to a two-state Markov chain. Also shown is the exit of one particle through the open gate.

Figure 2: Plots of π(2)(x0; xr) (global resetting) and π(2) loc(x0; xr) (local resetting) as functions of the resetting rate r, which are given by equations (4.9)–(4.10)) and (4.16), respectively. We take x0 = (x, x) and xr = (xr, xr) with (a) x = 0.1, xr = 0.9 and (b) x = 0.9, xr = 0.1. The solid curves correspond to π(2) loc, whereas the discrete points sample π(2). We set L = 1 and truncate the double summation of the Green’s function in (4.3) by taking 1 ≤n, m ≤N with N = 1000.

Figure 3: Corresponding plots of π(2)(x0; xr) and π(2) loc(x0; xr) as functions of the initial position x with x0 = (x, x) and xr = (xr, xr). The solid curve corresponds to π(2) loc, whereas the discrete points sample π(2). We also set L = 1, r = 100 and N = 1000. The inset shows π(2) for N = 1000 (solid red circle) and N = 1100 (indicated by x) and π(2) loc (solid black circle) for x = 0.5.

Figure 4: Schematic diagram comparing global versus local gating. (a) Global gating: Particles diﬀuse in a common switching environment consisting of a stochastically gated boundary at x = L. (b) Local gating: each particle independently switches between two conﬁgurations, only one of which can exit the boundary at x = L. (2D representation is for illustrative purposes.)

Figure 5: (a) Plot of the splitting probability π(2)(x, x) for a pair of Brownian particles with the same initial position x. The Fourier series solution (A.26) is truncated after 200 terms. In addition, a 50,000-dimensional version of the inﬁnite-dimensional matrix equation (A.30) is solved to estimate the Fourier coeﬃcients. Other parameters are L = 1 and ξ = p
